hook.scenario: Imagine two developers: Dev A can finish an API in 10 days, Dev B in 15 days. If they pair program, how fast does it ship? This is the exact math tech companies test you on.

mental_model.intuition: Work = Time x Rate. Instead of adding days, add their per-day speed (rates).
mental_model.description: Never add time directly. If A takes 10 days and B takes 15 days, they do not take 25 days together. Convert everything to 'work done per day' (efficiency) and add those rates.

pattern_discovery.setup: Calculate A and B's combined rate, then find time.
pattern_discovery.prompt: Why do you add rates (1/10 + 1/15) and not times (10 + 15)?
pattern_discovery.rule: To find combined time, add their individual rates (reciprocals of time), then flip it back. Rates add; times do not.

formula.statement: If A takes x days and B takes y days, together they take (x x y) / (x + y) days. Rate of A = 1/x per day. Work done in t days = t / x. For 3+ workers: use LCM method - assume total work = LCM of their individual times, assign each worker their units per day, sum the rates. If A is k times as efficient as B, A takes time = B's time / k. Work remaining after t days of one worker = 1 - (t/x).
formula.explain: The formula (xy)/(x+y) is the algebraic simplification of 1/(1/x + 1/y). For 3+ people, the LCM method is faster: set total work = LCM of given days, compute each person's daily output, sum outputs, then divide total by combined daily output.

worked_example.problem: A can complete a piece of work in 12 days. B is 50% more efficient than A. How long do they take to finish together?
steps: A's rate = 1/12 work per day. B is 50% more efficient: B's rate = 1.5 x (1/12) = 1/8 per day.
Sanity-check via LCM method: LCM(12, 8) = 24 units. A does 2 units/day, B does 3 units/day. Together 5 units/day. Time = 24/5 = 4.8 days. Matches.

worked_example.problem: X and Y together finish a job in 6 days. X alone takes 10 days. After X and Y work together for 3 days, X leaves. How many more days will Y take to finish?
steps: Work done in 3 days together = 3 x (1/6) = 1/2.
Y alone does 1/15 per day. Time for Y to finish 1/2 = (1/2) / (1/15) = 15/2 = 7.5 days.

shortcuts.title: The LCM (Chocolate) Method
shortcuts.trick: Assume total work = LCM of given days. Calculate work units done per day by each person.
shortcuts.example: A=10 days, B=15 days. LCM=30 chocolates. A eats 3/day, B eats 2/day. Together 5/day. 30/5 = 6 days.

common_mistakes.title: Adding Days Directly
common_mistakes.explanation: Assuming A(10 days) + B(15 days) = 25 days. More workers means LESS time, not more. Always add rates, not times.

recall_questions.q: If P takes 20 days and Q takes 30 days, what is their combined time using the formula (x*y)/(x+y)?
What is the assumed total work (LCM) for workers taking 12, 15, and 20 days?
If A completes 1/3 of the work in 5 days, how many days for the full work?

oa_questions.question: A can do a piece of work in 8 days. B is 3 times as efficient as A. In how many days can they complete it together?
oa_questions.approach: LCM method: total work = 8 units. A does 1 unit/day, B does 3 units/day (3x efficient). Together = 4 units/day. Time = 8/4 = 2 days.

oa_questions.question: A takes 15 days and B takes 20 days. They work together for 4 days, then A leaves. How many days will B take to finish the remaining work?
oa_questions.approach: LCM(15,20) = 60 units. A=4/day, B=3/day. In 4 days together: (4+3)*4 = 28 units done. Remaining = 32 units. B takes 32/3 = 10.67 days.
